topic: IMCI danger signs in a sick child
clinicalStem: A 14-month-old boy is brought to a rural health centre by his mother. He has had fever for 4 days, has been vomiting repeatedly, and has not been able to drink or breastfeed since this morning. On examination he is lethargic, his temperature is 38.9°C, respiratory rate is 44 breaths/min, and he has no lower-chest wall indrawing. The mother says he had a convulsion at home an hour ago. According to the IMCI framework, which of the following best classifies this child's condition and directs the most urgent action?
options: Fast breathing pneumonia — start oral amoxicillin and review in 2 days
Very severe febrile disease — refer urgently to hospital after pre-referral treatment
Severe malnutrition — initiate therapeutic feeding and observe
Uncomplicated malaria — give oral artemether-lumefantrine and discharge
Ear infection — prescribe topical antibiotic eardrops and reassure
explanation: The child has multiple IMCI general danger signs: convulsion, inability to drink/feed, and lethargy. Any single general danger sign classifies the child as having a very severe febrile disease requiring urgent referral and pre-referral treatment (e.g., rectal artesunate/antibiotics depending on setting). Fast-breathing pneumonia classification requires no danger signs and stable feeding. Discharge or observation is inappropriate.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Fast breathing pneumonia applies only when there are no general danger signs and the child can feed; this child cannot feed and had a convulsion, disqualifying this classification.
Correct. Inability to feed/drink, convulsion, and lethargy are IMCI general danger signs; any one of these mandates urgent referral after initiating pre-referral treatment.
Severe malnutrition is not supported by the vignette; even if present it would still require referral, but the immediate driver here is general danger signs from acute febrile illness.
Uncomplicated malaria cannot be assigned when general danger signs are present; oral treatment and discharge would be dangerous.
There are no ear findings; more critically, general danger signs override any lower-acuity diagnosis and demand urgent referral.
clinicalPearl: Any single IMCI general danger sign (unable to drink/feed, convulsions, vomiting everything, or lethargic/unconscious) mandates immediate referral — do not treat and discharge.
keyClue: Convulsion at home + inability to drink/feed + lethargy = IMCI general danger signs
commonExamTrap: Focusing on fast breathing (RR 44) and misclassifying as simple pneumonia, ignoring the general danger signs that override all other classification
missedConcept: IMCI general danger signs always take priority over organ-specific classification and require urgent referral
redFlagReason: Child has multiple IMCI general danger signs including convulsion and inability to feed; missing this classification and failing to refer urgently risks death

clinicalStem: A 10-month-old girl is brought to a community health post with cough for 3 days. Her mother says she is feeding well and has had no convulsions. Temperature is 37.4°C, respiratory rate is 52 breaths/min, and there is no lower-chest wall indrawing. No stridor at rest, no general danger signs. According to the IMCI guidelines, which is the most appropriate classification and first-line outpatient antibiotic?
options: No pneumonia: cough or cold — supportive care only, soothe throat with safe remedy
Severe pneumonia — urgent hospital referral with parenteral benzylpenicillin
Pneumonia — treat with oral amoxicillin for 5 days
Very severe disease — give pre-referral artesunate and refer
Stridor — give inhaled salbutamol and reassure
explanation: A child aged 2–11 months with RR ≥50 breaths/min (or ≥40 for 12–59 months) and no lower-chest wall indrawing and no general danger signs meets the IMCI criteria for pneumonia (not severe). First-line treatment in IMCI guidelines is oral amoxicillin for 5 days. No referral is needed if the child feeds well and can follow up.
wrongAnswerExplanations: No pneumonia requires RR below the age-specific threshold; RR 52 in a 10-month-old is above the threshold of 50, so pneumonia classification applies.
Severe pneumonia requires lower-chest wall indrawing or stridor; neither is present here.
Correct. RR 52 exceeds the threshold for a 2–11-month-old (≥50), with no danger signs or chest indrawing — this is IMCI pneumonia treated with oral amoxicillin.
No general danger signs or malaria indicators are present; very severe disease classification is not warranted.
No stridor is documented; the stridor option is a distractor unrelated to this presentation.
clinicalPearl: IMCI fast-breathing threshold: ≥60 breaths/min for <2 months; ≥50 for 2–11 months; ≥40 for 12–59 months — memorise these cut-offs.
keyClue: RR 52 in a 10-month-old with no indrawing and no danger signs = IMCI pneumonia
commonExamTrap: Using the wrong age-specific RR cut-off (e.g., applying 40 instead of 50 for this age group) and misclassifying as no pneumonia
missedConcept: Fast breathing thresholds in IMCI are age-specific; using the wrong threshold leads to under- or over-diagnosis

clinicalStem: A 2-year-old boy is brought to a primary health centre by his grandmother. He has had diarrhoea for 10 days and has not been gaining weight. On examination he is apathetic, has bilateral pitting oedema of the feet, and visible severe wasting. His mid-upper-arm circumference (MUAC) is 10.5 cm. He has no acute respiratory distress. Which single investigation at the point-of-care best determines the immediate management pathway for this child according to IMCI/WHO protocols?
options: Chest X-ray to rule out tuberculosis
Stool microscopy for ova and parasites
Blood glucose measurement to detect hypoglycaemia
Mantoux test for latent TB
Serum albumin level to quantify protein deficiency
explanation: Children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) — defined by MUAC <11.5 cm, bilateral oedema, or visible severe wasting — are at very high risk of hypoglycaemia, which is a leading cause of early death. Bedside blood glucose is the critical point-of-care investigation that directly drives immediate management (50% glucose or breastfeeding if hypoglycaemic). Other investigations are secondary.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Chest X-ray may be warranted later to exclude TB or pneumonia but is not the single most urgent POC investigation that changes immediate management.
Stool microscopy is useful for chronic diarrhoea workup but does not change the immediate life-threatening risk of hypoglycaemia in SAM.
Correct. Hypoglycaemia is the most immediately life-threatening complication of SAM; bedside blood glucose guides whether to give emergency glucose before any other intervention.
Mantoux test is unreliable in SAM due to immunosuppression and is not the immediate investigation needed.
Serum albumin confirms protein deficiency but is not a POC test and does not alter emergency management in the first hours.
clinicalPearl: Always check blood glucose immediately in any child with SAM — hypoglycaemia is silent and lethal, and treating it costs nothing.
keyClue: Bilateral pitting oedema + MUAC 10.5 cm + apathy = SAM → hypoglycaemia risk
commonExamTrap: Ordering serum albumin or X-ray first, missing that hypoglycaemia is the immediately reversible killer in SAM
missedConcept: SAM management follows the WHO ten-step protocol; hypoglycaemia screening is step one because it is rapidly fatal and easily treated
redFlagReason: Hypoglycaemia in severe acute malnutrition can cause sudden death; missing this investigation and delaying glucose treatment is immediately life-threatening

topic: Malaria diagnosis in a febrile child
clinicalStem: A 4-year-old girl in a malaria-endemic rural district presents with 3 days of high fever, rigors, headache, and vomiting. She is fully conscious, feeding reasonably, and has no signs of severe disease. Her temperature is 39.2°C, spleen tip is palpable, and respiratory rate is 38 breaths/min. The clinic has no microscopy but has a stock of malaria rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs). Which is the best next investigation to confirm the diagnosis before initiating treatment?
options: Thick and thin blood film for Plasmodium species identification
Full blood count to assess for anaemia and thrombocytopaenia
Malaria rapid diagnostic test (RDT) for Plasmodium falciparum HRP-2 antigen
Urine dipstick to exclude urinary tract infection as an alternative cause
Chest X-ray to rule out pneumonia as a co-existing cause of fever
explanation: In resource-limited endemic settings without microscopy, the WHO and national malaria guidelines recommend parasitological confirmation before treatment using a malaria RDT. The HRP-2-based RDT detects P. falciparum antigen rapidly at point of care. Blood film remains the gold standard but is unavailable here. Empirical treatment without testing is no longer recommended in school-age children who can tolerate a clinical assessment.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Thick and thin blood film is the gold standard but is not available at this clinic; the RDT is the appropriate alternative in this setting.
FBC is supportive (anaemia, thrombocytopaenia suggest malaria) but does not confirm Plasmodium infection and should not replace parasitological diagnosis.

Correct. RDT for P. falciparum HRP-2 antigen is the recommended point-of-care test when microscopy is unavailable; WHO mandates confirmation before treatment in children over 5 years (and practice favours it in younger children in stable presentations).
Urine dipstick may exclude UTI but does not address the primary diagnosis of malaria in an endemic area.
Chest X-ray is not indicated without respiratory signs; RR 38 in a 4-year-old is below the IMCI pneumonia threshold and in context of fever is likely fever-driven tachypnoea.
clinicalPearl: WHO policy: test before treating — use RDT in the absence of microscopy; empirical anti-malarial treatment without parasitological confirmation promotes drug resistance.
keyClue: Endemic area + no microscopy available + stable child with fever and splenomegaly = use RDT
commonExamTrap: Choosing blood film (gold standard but unavailable) rather than the best available alternative (RDT)
missedConcept: Best investigation means best available and appropriate in context, not theoretical gold standard if it is unavailable

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old university student presents to a peri-urban clinic with 4 days of high fever, severe retro-orbital headache, myalgia, and a faint macular rash that appeared on day 3. He returned from a coastal city 6 days ago. Examination: T 38.7°C, BP 108/70 mmHg, HR 102 bpm. There is mild hepatomegaly and a positive tourniquet test. FBC: WBC 2.8 × 10⁹/L, platelets 88 × 10⁹/L, haematocrit 44%. Which single investigation would most specifically confirm the diagnosis at this stage of illness?
options: Malaria RDT for P. falciparum HRP-2 antigen
NS1 antigen detection test for dengue virus
IgM antibody for dengue by ELISA
Blood culture for Salmonella typhi
Thick blood film for trypanosomes
explanation: This is day 4 of dengue illness; the NS1 antigen is detectable from day 1 through approximately day 9 and is the most sensitive specific test in the early (febrile) phase. Dengue IgM rises from day 4–5 but may not yet be detectable with certainty at day 4 in a primary infection. The clinical picture (positive tourniquet test, leukopenia, thrombocytopaenia, retro-orbital headache, rash) strongly suggests dengue.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Malaria RDT is appropriate for Plasmodium infection; the clinical constellation of leukopenia, thrombocytopaenia, positive tourniquet test, and retro-orbital headache is far more consistent with dengue than malaria.
Correct. NS1 antigen is the best early-phase (days 1–9) confirmatory test for dengue; it is specific, rapid, and available in most endemic-area clinics.
Dengue IgM is useful from day 5 onwards and in convalescence; on day 4 it may still be negative in a primary infection, making NS1 more reliable at this stage.
Blood culture for Salmonella typhi investigates typhoid, which shares fever but not the thrombocytopaenia, leukopenia, rash pattern, and positive tourniquet test seen here.
Trypanosomes cause African sleeping sickness (tsetse fly exposure, chancre, lymphadenopathy) — this student's presentation and coastal travel history do not fit.
clinicalPearl: In dengue, NS1 antigen peaks in the febrile phase (days 1–9); pair with dengue IgM/IgG if the patient presents after day 5 for highest sensitivity.
keyClue: Day 4 fever + positive tourniquet test + thrombocytopaenia + leukopenia + retro-orbital headache = early dengue → NS1 antigen
commonExamTrap: Ordering IgM ELISA which may be negative this early, or diagnosing malaria without parasitological evidence when dengue features are dominant
missedConcept: Timing matters in dengue serology: NS1 antigen for early phase, IgM/IgG for later phase or seroconversion confirmation

clinicalStem: A 6-year-old boy from a malaria-endemic area is brought to a district health centre unconscious. His mother reports 2 days of fever followed by two generalised tonic-clonic convulsions at home. He has not been able to drink since yesterday. Temperature is 40.1°C, GCS is 8/15, respiratory rate is 42 breaths/min with deep breathing (Kussmaul pattern). Malaria RDT is positive for P. falciparum. Blood glucose at point of care is 2.1 mmol/L. Which of the following best describes the most critical diagnosis driving immediate management?
options: Uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria with febrile convulsions
Cerebral malaria with hypoglycaemia — a WHO-defined severe malaria emergency
Bacterial meningitis mimicking malaria — lumbar puncture required before treatment
Dengue haemorrhagic fever with cerebral involvement
Status epilepticus from a primary seizure disorder with incidental parasitaemia
explanation: This child meets multiple WHO criteria for severe P. falciparum malaria: impaired consciousness (GCS 8, cerebral malaria), repeated convulsions, inability to drink, and hypoglycaemia (blood glucose <2.2 mmol/L). This is a combined emergency requiring immediate IV/IM artesunate, correction of hypoglycaemia with 10% dextrose, and urgent referral. Uncomplicated malaria does not involve coma or hypoglycaemia.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Uncomplicated malaria cannot be assigned when the child is unconscious (GCS 8) and hypoglycaemic; this is severe malaria by WHO criteria.

Correct. Coma + positive P. falciparum RDT + hypoglycaemia + convulsions = cerebral malaria with hypoglycaemia; this is the most critical composite diagnosis requiring parenteral artesunate and glucose immediately.

Bacterial meningitis is a reasonable differential but should not delay anti-malarial treatment in a confirmed positive RDT setting; treat both empirically if in doubt, but the RDT positivity and malaria endemicity make cerebral malaria the primary diagnosis.
Dengue with cerebral involvement is unusual and does not match the positive P. falciparum RDT result.
Primary epilepsy with incidental parasitaemia does not explain Kussmaul breathing, hypoglycaemia, and sustained unconsciousness; this is not a benign coincidence.
clinicalPearl: Cerebral malaria (GCS <11 in a child) + hypoglycaemia is a double emergency: give IV artesunate AND 10% dextrose simultaneously — delay kills.
keyClue: GCS 8 + positive P. falciparum RDT + blood glucose 2.1 mmol/L + Kussmaul breathing = severe malaria with cerebral and metabolic involvement
commonExamTrap: Stopping to perform a lumbar puncture before treating when malaria is confirmed; or classifying as uncomplicated because 'it's just malaria'
missedConcept: WHO severe malaria criteria include impaired consciousness, repeated convulsions, hypoglycaemia, and acidotic breathing — any one mandates parenteral artesunate
redFlagReason: Cerebral malaria with hypoglycaemia is immediately life-threatening; delayed parenteral treatment and glucose correction are associated with rapid death or neurological sequelae

clinicalStem: A 3-year-old unvaccinated girl presents with a 3-day history of high fever, runny nose, red eyes, and a harsh cough. On day 4, small white spots with a red base are noted on the buccal mucosa bilaterally. Her mother reports that a classmate at the same nursery had a similar rash illness 2 weeks ago. On day 5 a maculopapular rash begins behind the ears and spreads to the face and trunk. Which is the most specific clinical finding that confirms the diagnosis before the rash appears?
options: Bilateral conjunctivitis with photophobia
Coryza, cough, and fever (the 3 Cs)
Koplik spots on the buccal mucosa
Maculopapular rash starting behind the ears
Cervical lymphadenopathy with low-grade fever
explanation: Koplik spots — tiny bluish-white spots with an erythematous base on the buccal mucosa opposite the lower molars — are pathognomonic of measles. They appear 1–2 days before the rash and disappear within 2 days of its onset, making them the most specific finding for early diagnosis before the rash. The 3 Cs (cough, coryza, conjunctivitis) are prodromal but non-specific.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Bilateral conjunctivitis occurs in measles but also in adenovirus, chlamydia, and other viral infections — it is not specific.
The 3 Cs are characteristic of the measles prodrome but are shared by influenza, RSV, and other respiratory viruses, making them non-specific.
Correct. Koplik spots are pathognomonic for measles; their presence before the rash allows early clinical diagnosis and isolation.
The maculopapular rash starting behind the ears is characteristic but appears after Koplik spots and is also seen in rubella and roseola, making it less specific than Koplik spots.
Cervical lymphadenopathy with low-grade fever is more characteristic of rubella (post-auricular and suboccipital nodes) than measles.
clinicalPearl: Koplik spots are pathognomonic for measles — spot them on day 2–4 of prodrome before the rash to diagnose early and initiate isolation.
keyClue: White spots with red base on buccal mucosa 1 day before the rash = Koplik spots = measles
commonExamTrap: Choosing the maculopapular rash as the most specific feature, when Koplik spots are pathognomonic and appear earlier
missedConcept: Koplik spots are the only pathognomonic clinical sign in measles and appear before the rash — knowing this allows pre-rash diagnosis

clinicalStem: A newborn boy is assessed at 3 days of age. His mother, aged 22, had no antenatal care and reported a mild rash illness with joint pains at 10 weeks of gestation. The baby has bilateral cataracts, a harsh pansystolic murmur (consistent with patent ductus arteriosus on echocardiography), and a purpuric rash described by the team as 'blueberry muffin' in appearance. Birth weight is 2.1 kg. Hearing screen is failed bilaterally. Which investigation would most specifically confirm the underlying aetiology?
options: VDRL/RPR for congenital syphilis
Cytomegalovirus (CMV) urine PCR
Rubella-specific IgM from neonatal serum
Toxoplasma IgM from neonatal serum
Karyotype for chromosomal anomaly
explanation: The clinical triad of cataracts, congenital heart disease (PDA/pulmonary artery stenosis), and sensorineural hearing loss, alongside blueberry muffin rash (extramedullary haematopoiesis) and maternal first-trimester rash illness, is classic for congenital rubella syndrome (CRS). Neonatal rubella-specific IgM (which does not cross the placenta) confirms intrauterine infection. Rubella IgG from the mother could represent past immunity, but IgM in the neonate is diagnostic.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Congenital syphilis causes snuffles, hepatosplenomegaly, periostitis, and skin lesions but not the classic triad of cataracts + PDA/PS + deafness; VDRL is not the most specific test here.

CMV is the most common congenital infection and causes microcephaly, periventricular calcifications, hepatosplenomegaly, and hearing loss, but cataracts and PDA are not typical; CMV PCR is not the best first test given this specific clinical constellation.
Correct. Rubella-specific IgM in the neonate confirms CRS; the triad (cataract, CHD, hearing loss) + maternal first-trimester rash illness is pathognomonic for rubella.
Congenital toxoplasmosis causes chorioretinitis, hydrocephalus, intracranial calcifications, and seizures but not cataracts or PDA; toxoplasma IgM is not the best test here.
Chromosomal anomalies (e.g., trisomy 21) can cause CHD and cataracts but not blueberry muffin rash, deafness of this pattern, or the maternal illness history.
clinicalPearl: The congenital rubella triad = cataracts + congenital heart disease (PDA/pulmonary stenosis) + sensorineural deafness; maternal first-trimester rash infection confirms the timing.
keyClue: Maternal first-trimester rash + neonatal cataracts + PDA + deafness + blueberry muffin rash = congenital rubella syndrome
commonExamTrap: Confusing with CMV (most common TORCH) and ordering CMV PCR, missing the specific rubella triad and the diagnostic IgM test
missedConcept: Congenital rubella syndrome has a pathognomonic triad; rubella IgM in the neonate (not IgG) is the confirming test because IgM does not cross the placenta

clinicalStem: A 4-year-old malnourished boy from a low-income urban settlement presents on day 5 of a measles illness. He now has corneal haziness in the right eye and photophobia. His mother reports he has been eating poorly for months. His weight-for-age z-score is −2.8. Which single investigation at primary care level would most directly guide the urgency and dose of vitamin A supplementation in this child?
options: Serum retinol level to quantify vitamin A deficiency precisely
Slit-lamp examination by an ophthalmologist to stage corneal involvement
Clinical assessment of the eye using a torch, combined with the age and nutritional history
Visual acuity testing using Snellen chart
Fundoscopic examination to assess for papilloedema
explanation: WHO guidelines for vitamin A supplementation during measles are based on clinical criteria: all children with measles should receive vitamin A; those with signs of vitamin A deficiency (xerophthalmia, corneal ulceration) or severe malnutrition receive a third dose the next day. In a primary care setting, clinical eye examination with a torch (to detect corneal haziness, ulceration) combined with nutritional status and age is the practical investigation that determines dose and urgency. Serum retinol is not a point-of-care test and does not change acute management.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Serum retinol is a research/reference lab test, not available at primary care level, and WHO management protocols do not require it before giving vitamin A.

Slit-lamp exam by a specialist is definitive but is not available at primary care and would cause harmful delay; clinical torch examination is sufficient to identify corneal involvement requiring urgent vitamin A.

Correct. Clinical torch examination of the eye plus age and nutritional status is the primary care standard; WHO guidelines base vitamin A dosing on these clinical findings, making specialist testing unnecessary before treatment.
Visual acuity assessment is important but cannot be reliably performed in a 4-year-old with photophobia and does not change the immediate decision to give vitamin A.
Fundoscopy looks for intracranial hypertension, which is not the clinical question here; it would not guide vitamin A dosing.
clinicalPearl: In measles, do not wait for serum retinol — give vitamin A to all children clinically; a second dose the next day if corneal signs or malnutrition are present.
keyClue: Measles + corneal haziness + malnutrition = clinical signs sufficient to give high-dose vitamin A immediately
commonExamTrap: Ordering serum retinol (unavailable at primary care, delays treatment) rather than using clinical findings to guide the proven WHO protocol
missedConcept: Vitamin A in measles is a clinical decision based on bedside findings — serum levels are not required and should not delay treatment

clinicalStem: A 3-year-old boy in an under-immunised community presents with a 2-day history of fever, irritability, and muscle pain, followed by sudden onset of asymmetric flaccid weakness of the left leg with absent knee jerk. There is no sensory deficit. He received one dose of oral polio vaccine at 6 weeks but missed subsequent doses. Which investigation is most essential to confirm the aetiological diagnosis and fulfil national surveillance requirements?
options: MRI of the lumbar spine to differentiate spinal cord pathology
Nerve conduction studies to classify the neuropathy type
Two stool samples collected 24–48 hours apart for poliovirus isolation
Cerebrospinal fluid analysis for cell count and protein
Serum poliovirus IgG antibody titres
explanation: WHO polio surveillance mandates stool specimen collection for virological confirmation in every case of acute flaccid paralysis (AFP) in children under 15. Two specimens are collected 24–48 hours apart, ideally within 14 days of paralysis onset, and sent to a WHO-accredited laboratory for poliovirus isolation and intratypic differentiation (wild vs vaccine-derived). This is the gold standard confirmatory test and a public health reporting requirement.
wrongAnswerExplanations: MRI is useful to exclude transverse myelitis or Guillain-Barré but does not confirm poliovirus infection; it should not replace virological testing.
Nerve conduction studies characterise the lesion as anterior horn cell vs peripheral nerve but do not identify poliovirus; they are supplementary, not confirmatory.

Correct. Two stool specimens for poliovirus isolation are the WHO-mandated gold-standard investigation for AFP surveillance; they confirm wild poliovirus or vaccine-derived poliovirus and trigger outbreak response.
CSF in polio typically shows pleocytosis (lymphocytic) and mildly elevated protein; this is supportive but non-specific and does not confirm poliovirus.
Serum IgG antibody titres reflect past immunity or vaccination; they cannot confirm acute infection and are not the surveillance gold standard.
clinicalPearl: Every child under 15 with AFP must have two stool samples collected 24–48 hours apart — this is a global polio surveillance obligation and the only way to confirm or exclude poliovirus.
keyClue: Asymmetric flaccid paralysis + absent reflexes + no sensory loss + under-vaccinated child = AFP requiring stool poliovirus isolation
commonExamTrap: Choosing CSF analysis as the confirming test when stool virological culture is the WHO-required gold standard for both diagnosis and surveillance
missedConcept: Polio diagnosis requires virological confirmation from stool, not CSF; this distinction is critical for surveillance and outbreak response

clinicalStem: A 5-year-old girl presents 10 days after a febrile diarrhoeal illness with progressive ascending weakness. She now has symmetrical bilateral leg weakness with absent ankle and knee jerks, but also absent upper limb reflexes. There is no fever currently. Glove-and-stocking sensory loss is present. CSF shows protein 1.2 g/L and cell count of 4 cells/µL (albuminocytological dissociation). Stool poliovirus PCR is negative. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Wild-type poliovirus infection — paralytic poliomyelitis
Vaccine-associated paralytic poliomyelitis (VAPP)
Guillain-Barré syndrome (acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy)
Transverse myelitis from viral infection
Botulism from ingested toxin
explanation: The combination of ascending symmetric paralysis with sensory loss, areflexia, post-infectious onset (10 days after diarrhoeal illness, commonly Campylobacter), albuminocytological dissociation (high protein, normal cells) in the CSF, and negative stool poliovirus PCR is classic for Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS). Polio produces asymmetric purely motor paralysis with pleocytosis in CSF; sensory signs and albuminocytological dissociation point away from polio.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Wild-type polio causes asymmetric, purely motor flaccid paralysis without sensory loss; the symmetry, sensory signs, and albuminocytological dissociation argue strongly against it.
VAPP presents similarly to wild-type polio (asymmetric motor, no sensory loss, CSF pleocytosis) and would not explain sensory deficits or albuminocytological dissociation.

Correct. GBS classically presents as ascending symmetric weakness with sensory involvement, areflexia, albuminocytological dissociation (high CSF protein, normal WBC), and post-infectious onset — all present here, with negative poliovirus stool PCR.

Transverse myelitis causes a spinal level (sensory level), sphincter disturbance, and upper motor neuron signs below the lesion; it does not cause areflexia at all levels or albuminocytological dissociation.
Botulism causes descending paralysis beginning with cranial nerve involvement (diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia), not ascending limb weakness with sensory loss.
clinicalPearl: Symmetric ascending paralysis + sensory loss + albuminocytological dissociation + post-infectious = GBS; asymmetric purely motor flaccid paralysis + CSF pleocytosis + fever = think polio.
keyClue: Symmetric ascending paralysis + sensory loss + CSF albuminocytological dissociation + negative poliovirus PCR = Guillain-Barré syndrome
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing polio because both cause flaccid paralysis; the symmetry, sensory involvement, and CSF findings are the key differentiating features
missedConcept: Polio is purely motor and asymmetric with CSF pleocytosis; GBS is motor + sensory, symmetric, with albuminocytological dissociation — these differences are diagnostic

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman presents on day 5 of dengue (confirmed by NS1 antigen on day 2) with sudden defervescence and onset of abdominal pain, repeated vomiting, and gum bleeding. Her temperature is now 37.1°C, BP 92/60 mmHg, HR 118 bpm, and she appears anxious with cold extremities. Her haematocrit has risen from 36% to 50% over 24 hours. Platelet count is 32 × 10⁹/L. Which single clinical and laboratory finding is most diagnostic of the critical phase of severe dengue with plasma leakage?
options: Thrombocytopaenia alone (platelet count <100 × 10⁹/L)
Positive tourniquet test with petechiae
Rising haematocrit (≥20% increase from baseline) with hypotension and cold extremities
NS1 antigen positivity on repeat testing
Leukopenia (WBC <4 × 10⁹/L)
explanation: A haematocrit rise of ≥20% from baseline is the WHO diagnostic criterion for plasma leakage in severe dengue. Combined with hypotension (BP 92/60), tachycardia, cold extremities, and shock occurring at defervescence (the critical phase transition around day 4–6), this constitutes dengue shock syndrome. Thrombocytopaenia and leukopenia are supportive but non-specific for plasma leakage and can occur in uncomplicated dengue.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Thrombocytopaenia is expected in dengue but does not specifically indicate plasma leakage; it occurs in both uncomplicated and severe dengue.
Positive tourniquet test indicates increased capillary fragility and is useful for screening but is not specific for the critical plasma-leakage phase.

Correct. Haematocrit rise ≥20% reflects haemoconcentration from plasma leakage into third spaces; combined with haemodynamic compromise, this defines dengue shock syndrome requiring fluid resuscitation.
NS1 antigen is used for early diagnosis (days 1–9) and repeating it on day 5 adds no useful information; it does not assess plasma leakage or disease severity.
Leukopenia is a typical dengue finding but is non-specific for plasma leakage and does not distinguish severe from uncomplicated dengue.
clinicalPearl: Monitor haematocrit daily in dengue; a ≥20% rise at defervescence (day 4–6) is the early warning of plasma leakage and impending shock — act before BP falls.
keyClue: Haematocrit rise 36% → 50% (39% increase) + defervescence + hypotension + cold extremities = dengue shock syndrome
commonExamTrap: Focusing on thrombocytopaenia or gum bleeding as the defining severe dengue finding, while the haematocrit rise (plasma leakage marker) is the WHO-defined discriminator
missedConcept: Haemoconcentration (haematocrit rise ≥20%) is the specific WHO criterion for plasma leakage in dengue; monitoring serial haematocrits is mandatory in the critical phase
redFlagReason: Dengue shock syndrome with haemodynamic compromise requires immediate IV fluid resuscitation; delayed recognition of plasma leakage leads to refractory shock and death
